document.title: Optimizing image quality and dose for digital radiography of paediatric extremities
standards: ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable) principle
Leitlinie der Bundesärztekammer zur Qualitätssicherung in der Röntgendiagnostik - Qualitätskriterien röntgendiagnostischer Untersuchungen

sourceSummary.oneLine: Scientific exhibit investigating x-ray tube voltage and filtration effects on image quality and absorbed dose for digital radiography of paediatric distal extremities.
sourceSummary.officialUse: May support clinical evaluation or dose optimization rationale for digital radiography, but the source states further clinical validation is needed before adopting changes for general clinical use.
keyPoints: The study investigated tube voltage and filtration effects on image quality and dose for digital radiography of paediatric distal extremities.
A phantom study used contrast-to-noise ratio as the image quality indicator and mean absorbed dose as the dose indicator.
For fixed mean absorbed dose, contrast-to-noise ratio increased as tube voltage decreased and reached its maximum at 40 kV.
For fixed contrast-to-noise ratio, mean absorbed dose was lowest at 40 kV with no added filter.
The authors concluded that lower tube voltages may provide a better image quality and patient dose balance for digital radiography, but further clinical validation is needed.

concepts.name: ALARA principle
concepts.evidence: The optimization of x-ray imaging parameters according to the ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable) principle remains a continuous challenge in radiology, in particular for paediatric examinations.

concepts.name: Digital radiography
concepts.evidence: Digital imaging provides new possibilities because of the wide dynamic range of digital detectors and the digital image processing possibilities [1,2].

concepts.name: Paediatric extremity radiography
concepts.evidence: The purpose of this study was to investigate the influence of x-ray tube voltage and filtration on image quality and dose for digital radiography of paediatric distal extremities

concepts.name: Contrast-to-noise ratio
concepts.evidence: the measured contrast-to noise ratio (CNR) was used as the indicator for image quality

concepts.name: Mean absorbed dose
concepts.evidence: the mean absorbed dose (MAD) - determined by a combination of measurement and simulation - was used as indicator for the dose to the phantom.

concepts.name: Additional filtration
concepts.evidence: a standard medical x-ray tube with an optional 1 mm Al/0.1 mm Cu pre-filter

evidence.claim: The document is a scientific exhibit from ECR 2012.
evidence.evidenceText: Poster No.: C-2037 Congress: ECR 2012 Type: Scientific Exhibit

evidence.claim: The study purpose was to evaluate tube voltage and filtration effects on image quality and dose in paediatric distal extremity digital radiography.
evidence.evidenceText: The purpose of this study was to investigate the influence of x-ray tube voltage and filtration on image quality and dose for digital radiography of paediatric distal extremities and to determine conditions that give the best balance of image quality and patient dose.
evidence.pageTarget: Paediatric Extremity Digital Radiography Dose Optimization

evidence.claim: The phantom study acquired images at tube voltages from 40 kV to 66 kV.
evidence.evidenceText: Images were acquired at various tube voltages in the range from 40 kV to 66 kV.

evidence.claim: The study setup used a standard medical x-ray tube, optional 1 mm Al/0.1 mm Cu pre-filter, a PMMA phantom with an aluminium detail contrast, and a CsI/a-Si digital flat-panel detector.
evidence.evidenceText: The set-up (Fig. 1 on page 3) consisted of: • a standard medical x-ray tube with an optional 1 mm Al/0.1 mm Cu pre-filter • a phantom consisting of 1 cm PMMA and an additional 1 mm Al detail contrast • a digital flat-panel x-ray detector (CsI /a-Si based)

evidence.claim: Contrast-to-noise ratio was used as the image quality indicator, and mean absorbed dose was used as the phantom dose indicator.
evidence.evidenceText: the measured contrast-to noise ratio (CNR) was used as the indicator for image quality whereas the mean absorbed dose (MAD) - determined by a combination of measurement and simulation - was used as indicator for the dose to the phantom.

evidence.claim: For fixed mean absorbed dose of 25 μGy, CNR increased with decreasing tube voltage and reached a maximum at 40 kV.
evidence.evidenceText: The CNR increases continuously for decreasing tube voltage and reaches its maximum at 40 kV, the lowest attainable tube voltage.
evidence.pageTarget: Tube Voltage and CNR in Paediatric Digital Radiography

evidence.claim: For fixed CNR of 26, mean absorbed dose was lowest at 40 kV and without added filter.
evidence.evidenceText: the mean absorbed dose (MAD) is lowest at 40 kV, the lowest investigated tube voltage, and using no added filter.
evidence.pageTarget: Dose Optimization in Paediatric Digital Radiography

evidence.claim: Current practice and guidelines cited by the authors prescribe 50 to 60 kV for distal extremity examinations of children.
evidence.evidenceText: While current practice and guidelines [6] prescribe tube voltages in the range of 50 to 60 kV for examinations of the distal extremities of children

evidence.claim: The authors state that further clinical validation is needed before adopting the phantom study changes for general clinical use.
evidence.evidenceText: Further clinical validation of the phantom study outcome are needed before the changes can be adopted for general clinical use.
evidence.pageTarget: Clinical Validation of Radiography Dose Optimization
